title: Day 23 Quiz — Production Readiness Check
description: Assess your readiness to refactor for production and defend architectural decisions before Day 24 mock interview. Passing means you know when to apply each optimization — failing means re-read that section.

questions.prompt: What is the first step before applying React.memo to a component?

options.text: Wrap every component in memo by default
Profile with React DevTools to confirm unnecessary re-renders
Install a performance library
Convert all components to class components

questions.explanation: Senior engineers measure before optimizing — Day 20 and Day 23 production standard.

questions.prompt: In a production refactor, you should apply useCallback to every event handler regardless of whether the child is memoized.
questions.explanation: useCallback has cost. Apply when passing stable refs to memoized children or effect deps — not everywhere.

questions.prompt: After refactor, a memoized child still re-renders every parent render. Why?
questions.correctOutput: New function reference each render — memo child sees changed props
questions.explanation: Inline handlers create new references. Fix with useCallback when MemoChild is wrapped in React.memo.

questions.prompt: Which belong in a production refactor checklist? Select all that apply.

options.text: Feature-based folder structure with colocated types
Introduce a new state library not covered in Weeks 1–3
Loading, error, and empty states on async flows
Keyboard-accessible interactive elements

questions.explanation: Production refactor improves existing patterns — no new libraries outside the curriculum.

questions.prompt: When explaining folder structure in an architecture review, what is the strongest rationale?

options.text: It matches a blog post I read
Colocation — code that changes together lives together
More folders always means better code
The interviewer prefers monorepos

questions.explanation: Colocation reduces cross-import churn and speeds onboarding — core architecture talking point.

questions.prompt: Use {{blank1}} for low-frequency global reads like theme. Use {{blank2}} for server state caching and invalidation.
questions.explanation: Context vs RTK Query is a core Week 3 architecture decision you must defend in interviews.
React.lazy + Suspense defers chunk load until the route is visited — code splitting from Day 20.

questions.prompt: In one sentence: why document trade-offs in an architecture discussion?
questions.sampleAnswer: Senior engineers show they considered alternatives and understand what they sacrificed — not that every choice was perfect.
questions.explanation: Trade-off articulation separates mid-level from senior in project discussion rounds.

questions.prompt: RTK Query vs manual fetch in useEffect for a product list — production refactor choice?

options.text: Always manual fetch — RTK Query is overkill
RTK Query when already on Redux — built-in cache, invalidation, loading/error
RTK Query replaces all client state
useEffect fetch is fine without error handling

questions.explanation: Week 3 pattern — RTK Query owns server state lifecycle in production apps.

questions.prompt: Best first action in a production refactor?

options.text: Rewrite the entire app in a new framework
Audit current structure, list top 5 gaps, prioritize by interview impact
Add React.memo to every file before reading anything
Delete all existing code and start over

questions.explanation: Structured audit before refactor — same discipline as machine coding planning.
